speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is June 29th, 1995. The survivor being interviewed is Henry Dressler, also known as Heinz Dressler. The interviewer is Susan Douglass. The interview is taking place in Monmouth Beach, New jersey, in the United States. The language of the interview is English. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Today's date is June 29th, 1995. I am conducting an interview of Henry Dressler in Monmouth Beach, New Jersey, USA. My name is Susan Douglass-- S-U-S-A-N D-O-U-G-L-A-S-S. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Please state your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Henry Dressler. It's Henry-- H-E-N-R-Y. Dressler-- D as in David, R-E-S-S-L-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please state your name at birth and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry, my name at--

speaker: interviewer
text: Birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: At first?

speaker: interviewer
text: At birth. At first.

speaker: interviewee
text: At first. My name was Heinz Dressler. H-E-I-N-Z. Dressler. D-R-E-S-S-L-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know of.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in 1919.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you tell us the day--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --And month.

speaker: interviewee
text: October 22, 1919 in Dresden, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 75 years old now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please say and spell the names of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Joachim. That's J-0-A-C-H-I-M. He had a middle name that he rarely used-- Samuel, Dressler. D-R-E-S-S-L-E-R. My mother's name was Martha-- M-A-R-T-H-A-- Dressler.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many other children were there in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one sister. Her name was Susi, S-U-S-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born October 1st, 1914.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living in Dresden?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a business manufacturing domestic linen, tablecloth, things like this, mostly for department stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people worked for your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not know it-- I do not remember-- relatively small-- relatively small business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say five or six people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between five and 10, I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What role did your mother play in this business?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was active-- actively involved in it. She worked with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember most about the city of Dresden as you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Dresden was a very beautiful city. It had about 500,000 inhabitants, about 5,000 Jews, if I remember right. It was, in olden ages, was the seat of the Kingdom of Saxony. It had some beautiful buildings. It was a city that was well-known for its beauty, and it was a nice city to live in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember most about the house that you lived in as you were growing up in Dresden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was born in the house in which we-- I-- we lived in-- I lived there. As long as I lived in Dresden, I lived in this house. The address was 34 Struvestrasse. That's S-T-R-U-V-E-S-T-R-A-S-S-E. Struvestrasse. It was off one of the main streets of Dresden, which was Prager Strasse. P, as in Pete, like the city of Prague. P-A-R-A-G-E-R Strasse. It was a nice, residential street of mostly four-story apartment houses. That was more or less a norm for apartment-house buildings, and we lived on the second floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many rooms were there in your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had about four bed-- four-- four bedrooms, and there was a big dining room, a big living room, and a men's room, which we would call the library also. And we had three bathrooms. And we had a balcony in the front and in the back. It-- it was a nice apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How would you describe the religious life of your family in this period?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were normal. Not overly religious, and not-- not underly [LAUGHS] religiously observant. We went to temple at the holidays. Sometimes we went to temple on Friday night or Saturday. We were very conscious Jews. Our friends were mixed, and a lot of Jews in it. And I had Hebrew lessons, which was obligatory in-- in school. Usually one week-- one day a week on-- on Hebrew, and one day a week on Jewish history.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your home a kosher home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was not.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family celebrate some of the Jewish holidays, like Passover?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- my father's parents lived in Dresden, and Passover was a big holiday in their house, where all the family, some from Berlin, came over. It was a nice holiday and there were usually about 25, 30 people. And the big holidays, that's-- that's where we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember most of the Passover Seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first, I had a Seder [INAUDIBLE], of course, when I was younger. And then my grandfather-- who was at that time in-- in his 70s, but to us he was an old man with a little, white goatee, and they were a little hard of hearing-- and he sort of dominated the-- the table.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other family members, besides your grandparents, were you close to in Dresden?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had an-- had a brother. His name was Ferdinand, and I was sort of close to him. And he had another brother who died very young. I didn't really know him. And he had two sisters. One of them lived in Dresden. These three, four, we were-- we saw a lot. And while-- while my parents-- when I went to high school, my parents were on business-- I usually went from school by train-- by trolley-- to my grandparents' house, and I ate dinner or lunch, whatever you call it, there. So I spent a lot of time in their house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's move on to your education. Where did you go to school in Dresden?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was six years old, I went into what you would call your grammar school for four years. From there, I went to-- it was called [GERMAN] Gymnasium, the King George High School, which was a-- we called it in Germany a humanistic high school, dedicated to pre-college education. And the subjects where mostly more scientific. Our first language was English. It was sort of [INAUDIBLE] English. The second language would have been-- Latin. And the third language, Greek.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite subject in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ask me what were my un-favorite subjects in school. They are less.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your un-favorite subjects?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I loved-- I loved geography. I didn't like biology, which was biology-- the-- the-- in German it was the science of plants and trees and things that grow, not necessarily life. I didn't like zoology. I didn't like chemistry, but I liked physics. I liked Math. I liked arithmetic. I liked geography very, very much, and-- and-- language.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a favorite teacher during this period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in grammar school, we had only one teacher for the-- for the whole four years that I went there. Her name was Mrs. Richter, [LAUGHS] R-I-C-H-T-E-R. And-- and in high school, we had different teachers for different subjects. I don't think-- the system was a little different than it was here. No, I didn't have any-- any favorite teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point, before 1933, as you were in school--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what were your hopes for the future in terms of a career?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was the time that Hitler came to power in 1933, when I was 13 years old. So there-- it was open already. It would have been in the commercial field somewhere, or in the business field.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your education change once Hitler came to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after three years-- Hitler came to power in 1933-- after three years, I had to leave that high school because they would not have anymore Jews there, and I was admitted to a-- a private high school that was run by the Chamber of Commerce of Dresden. And that was a very much business-oriented high school. They started, back again, with English, but as a business language. And then it was English, French, and Spanish as business correspondence, and commercial arithmetic, rather than the-- the higher-- higher mathematics and-- and subjects-- bookkeeping, typing, short hand, and business law, and subjects that will help you in a-- in a education-- in a-- in a commercial field.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this period of time, did you-- in the '30s-- did you experience any antisemitism in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, there were some teachers-- yes. There was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had one teacher that taught biology, which was not one of my favorite subjects. And-- where I was always very good in school. I was always in the top one, two, or three in the class. And as a Jew, you could not be the first. They call it primus, the prime student because we sat in that order. I could not be the-- the-- the speaker for the class, so I was second or third, and I had all good ones or two grades. But in-- in biology I had a-- a four, the equivalent, whatever it was. And one day, the-- the biology teacher spoke up in the class and he comments that I got a bad grade because I was Jewish. He wanted to hear from me if this is true. His name was professor Bayer, B-A-Y-E-R. And of course, I had to get up and say, no, I am bad. But I was not a good student. But that was really the only thing that they did. And the other thing was that we had a lot of assemblies, and during Hitler time, of course, a lot of German songs, the German national anthem, and the and the other Hitler songs were being played. And I didn't like this too much. And where-- whenever you had to greet, you had to raise your arm. And so I played the kettle-- kettle drums in the school orchestra, and I had a good excuse not to have to stand up and raise my arm, sing with them. I just did my kettle-drums.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, once you went into this commercial school--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that was run by the chamber of commerce--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was this just for Jewish students, or was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: --For everyone.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were very few students. In high-- during my whole high school times, there was only about maybe two Jewish students in a class. That was all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was it. During this period of time in the '30s, what did you know of the Nazi party?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [LAUGHS] I couldn't really answer that question. We knew what-- we read the papers, of course, and we started hearing about the restrictions, about the things that started going on. I want to tell you, in the high school, there was a-- we were not allowed, as Jews, to go in the public swimming pool, even though this was built by a Jewish philanthropist in the city. We were excluded from-- from certain outings. Every year, they had a-- an annual like a-- like a little Olympic Games between all the-- between all the high schools. Each schools marched in the stadium, and it was a contest in the different subjects of-- of sports, different types of sports, and they marched around with our school colors. We were excluded to participate or even to watch. And as 12, 13 year olds, something, that's something that hurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can imagine. What did you do for recreation after school, since you were excluded from some of these activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a-- I loved to ride the bicycle. I did a lot of bicycle riding. I belonged to a Jewish sports club, [NON-ENGLISH] which I was not very active. I'm not a sports person. And I didn't really do too much. I was mostly at home. We had a very close family life, and that's where we spent out spare time. It was different than here. The children were with their parents much more at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was social life like for teenagers? Dating? Parties?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Germany, I could not have dated a non-Jewish girl. That was out of the question. I would run the danger of being-- being arrested, or worse. There were the racial laws where-- where Jews were not allowed to marry-- marry non-Jews, or vice versa, and it was very strict. And this was just out of the question. So I didn't have much of a social life like a 15 or 16 year old would have here, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: When Hitler came to power in 1933--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --this was after a very extensive election campaign--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what do you remember of that campaign in 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember-- after-- of the campaign I really do not remember too much. After he was elected, there was a big demonstration in Dresden. What do you call that? A [GERMAN], a torch light parade through the whole town. And everybody torch-lighted at night. And there must have been hundred thousands of people. And this, I remember. It was scary.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your parents react to the election of Hitler in 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were all concerned. We were all worried because-- but you know, whatever happened later, nobody had any idea that anything like this could happen. [INAUDIBLE] I had a-- a-- one [INAUDIBLE], my father had a brother in Berlin who was a-- who had just become a doctor, and who was married to a non-Jewish woman. And he just had graduated. And he put his shingle out on April 1st, 1933. That was three months after Hitler came to power, and it was a day in which all Jewish offices and all Jewish businesses and stores were boycotted. There was one SR man, the brownshirts, stood in front of the door. Here, this is a Jewish business. And the day he put out his shingle, his office was boycotted, and he was smart. He closed up his office the same day and went to Palestine. And-- but otherwise, you keep on living your life because you hope-- you hope for the best. You hope you'll keep on living and things will settle down and-- and straighten out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family ever consider immigrating from Germany in this period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we did. I think everybody did. But in order to do this-- it was not easy. The-- the-- America had the immigration quotas going by-- by place of birth, and they were-- they were very limited, and, like, the German quota was very tight. And you had to have somebody in the United States who would sponsor you. In other countries, some were-- most of them were difficult to get in. Shanghai was open. Cuba was open. I think Argentina was open. And there's one other thing you must not forget. If you live in a country-- you live all your life there, and you have your family there, and you have your business there, your friends there. You have your roots there. It is not easy to get yourself up and break up everything you have, only in fear of something worse happening, and start completely new in a country where you probably did not speak the language, where you tear apart the family. This is probably the most difficult thing for people. Not everybody can do this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides your uncle in Berlin who immigrated to Palestine, did any other members of your family immigrate in this period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. One of my father's sisters went to-- to Palestine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One of my father's brother went to Shanghai. Let me go up past-- [INAUDIBLE] may go up to '38. One of my mother's brothers went to Palestine. One of my mother's sisters-- brothers went to South America. And these were really the only people-- and that uncle doctor who went to Israel. These were on the real-- only people from our family who survived, who-- who were able to get out of Germany. All the others, they were gone. We have a couple of cousins left there, but from the direct uncles, this is all there was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your dad's business during the period from 1933 to '38?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in business-- he was in business. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a relatively small business, and he had his customers. And I have to tell you of an incident that happened. This was very strange. My father had a customer who was-- he lived out of town, a small town outside of Dresden, and he came in once a week in the morning. He brought in material, and he wanted some-- 100 bed sheets, or 300 table cloths, or what done, whatever it was. And he-- and he went back home, and my father and his people worked all day and night. And at midnight he went to the railroad station, and put the package on the train, and sent it to them. Well, he came down. I don't remember. It was either Erev Yom Kippur or Erev Rosh Hashanah. He didn't know we were Jewish. And he was a good customer, and you had to try to keep-- keep your customers. He brought in an order to make swastikas-- flags. He brought in a sample swastika and made-- gave him the order. You buy the material, you design it, you make it, you put it on the train tonight. And it was quite a difficult decision to do. First of all, we had never done this. And my father did it with-- with-- with tears in his eyes, but it was done. It's an experience we did not--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] ? What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must have been 1936 or 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father's business was never Aryanized?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was not. When we were thrown out of Germany, my father's sister, who-- who lived in Cologne, was visiting with us. And they-- she was staying with us. When they took us away, they-- they kept her there, and she closed everything up, and that-- that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned a number of restrictions on the Jewish community after 1933. For you, what was the toughest restriction on movement for Jews that you had to deal with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was young, and as a young fellow, you-- you don't take it that seriously. We could not go to the movies. Came a point where we could go to the movies. We could not go to a theater. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We could not go swimming. We could go swim in the river, but not-- not in a-- in a bath, in a public bath. And I joined a small social club. It got together once-- once in a-- once a month, just talking about different things. And all these club meetings had to be reported to the-- to the Gestapo, and for every club meeting, a civilian SS man or [INAUDIBLE] was present there to-- to see what we had to talk about it. And you were not free. You just were not free. There were other things that didn't personally affect me. There were a lot of arrests, and disappearances, and the first words about concentration camps became known. And-- but these were, at this point, still once removed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anyone who disappeared in this period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I do not remember. I don't. I probably-- I'm sure we knew some, but I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever feel that your life was in danger during this period of time before 1938?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I don't know. I am not aware of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. One of the events that occurred in Germany in 1936 was the Olympics. What do you recall of the Olympics in '36?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to high school there, and I was-- I loved geometry. So I loved to-- to-- to design the-- the five circles, and squares, and then the different colors, the-- interlock. I liked this very much. Otherwise, I was not-- never very much sports minded, and I-- so I have no special ideas about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you working in 1939-- 1938, rather.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I left high school in 1936 with the medium graduation level, and I got a job in a bank. It was a private, Jewish-owned bank in the center of town. I was an apprentice with a three-year contract from the chamber of commerce. That's the way they worked it. And I went through every department of the bank-- three months in this, then three months in this department. It was-- of course, we had no computers. We didn't even-- there was one adding machine in the whole bank that was the head bookkeeper. Even the-- the tellers had no adding machines. Everything was done by hand, and it's good brain training, by-- by the way. And I worked there until 1938. The bank was Aryanized, taken over by Germans, by a German bank. And I had to leave, like all the other Jewish people. And I got a job in a depart-- very fine department store in Dresden, mostly exclusively for women and some fine domestics. It had a fur department. It was very, very fine. I would compare it to Saks Fifth Avenue, but on a much smaller basis.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this department store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hirsch, H-I-R-S-C-H. Hirsch and Company. And they were in Prager Strasse, P-R-A-G-E-R Strasse, which was the main street that connected the railroad to the-- to the center of town.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did life change for you and your family in September of '38?

speaker: interviewee
text: September of '38, we were-- I do not exactly-- remember the exact dates. September, '38 we were-- my father-- my sister was engaged at that point to a fellow from Berlin, and he called her. There is going to be something. It's going to happen. But you-- you don't know what. My parents were still in business. I think it was September '29. I was home alone. The police knocked-- somebody knocked at the door. It was the police. You have to come to the police station. Well, I said, I have to wait for my parents. And they sat down with us and waited. And eating was always very important to me. When they came, I said, well, I have to eat dinner first. [LAUGHS] They waited, and then they left my aunt here, but they took us to the police station. And there were other Jews assembled, and they-- they took us to a large hall, like a ballroom hall, and we had to spend the night there. Then we were told we were going to be sent out next morning. And next morning, they put us all on a train. They took us by-- the police had these big open buses, these troop-- troop carriers, called it. They drove us through the whole town, so everybody could see us Jews being transported by the police, and they took us to the-- to the railroad station. And there was a train set aside for us, about 1,500 people, and 1,400 or 1,500 people from Dresden. And with the windows closed, they took us into-- towards the polish border. We found out later it was the Polish border. We arrived there in the middle of the night. They stopped the train. It was a-- it was a rainy, October night, and everybody had to get off the train. And they said, keep on. March on. And all of a sudden, we found out that we were in no man's land. The Germans had withdrawn. And some young fellows in our group, they marched on to find out where we were. And they came against-- across some Polish border patrols, and they didn't know what to do with us. And we had to stay there until next morning. And then finally, they got orders to let us in, and they took us to the nearest town. And from there, we were put on trains to different parts in-- in Poland.
